did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized deep learning convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for malignancy risk assessment and automated nevus counting in a dermatology context. Specifically, it employed 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM (using the DEXI score) and 3D-Vectra WB360 total body photography devices to classify pigmented skin lesions and detect melanoma in a high-risk population. The diagnostic performance of these AI systems was evaluated against dermatologists and histopathology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' on the first page. It describes a prospective, single-centre, observational study involving 143 high-risk patients and 1690 melanocytic lesions to evaluate the diagnostic performance of 2D and 3D CNNs against dermatologists and histopathology. It includes original methods, results (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC), and conclusions derived from primary data collection.

title: Diagnostic performance of augmented intelligence with 2D and 3D total body photography and convolutional neural networks in a high-risk population for melanoma under real-world conditions: A new era of skin cancer screening?
brief_summary: This prospective, single-centre study evaluated the diagnostic performance of 2D and 3D convolutional neural networks (CNNs) using total body photography against dermatologists for melanoma detection in 143 high-risk patients. The study assessed 1690 melanocytic lesions, with histopathology serving as the ground truth for 75 excised lesions. The 3D-CNN achieved a sensitivity of 90.0% and a specificity of 64.6%, while the 2D-CNN showed lower performance with 70% sensitivity and 40% specificity. Dermatologists and augmented intelligence (dermatologist plus AI) achieved comparable sensitivity to the 3D-CNN but superior specificity (92.3% and 86.2%, respectively). The study concludes that while the novel 3D-CNN device outperformed the 2D-CNN and matched dermatologist sensitivity, its low specificity and inaccurate automated nevus counting currently limit its clinical utility.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for detection and classification; 10 melanomas were included in the histopathology ground truth subset (n=75), comprising 2 in situ and 2 superficial spreading stage IA melanomas among false negatives.

The study assessed 1690 melanocytic lesions total. In the histopathology subset (n=75), 22 were classified as melanocytic naevus. The AI systems classified the majority (94.9%) of the 1690 lesions as benign.
Included in the histopathology ground truth subset (n=75), with 24 lesions classified as dysplastic naevus. Patients with dysplastic naevus syndrome were part of the high-risk inclusion criteria.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy risk assessment (benign vs. suspicious/melanoma) and automated nevus counting.
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates the diagnostic performance of 2D and 3D CNNs for classifying pigmented skin lesions as benign or suspicious (melanoma). The primary task is binary classification (benign vs. malignant/suspicious) using specific cut-offs (FotoFinder > 0.5, Vectra > 5.0). Additionally, the AI systems perform automated counting of nevi (a discrete count task), but the primary diagnostic evaluation focuses on classification accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC) against histopathology.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Single-Centre Cohort (University Hospital Basel)
datasets_used.description: A prospective, single-centre, observational study cohort recruited from the Department of Dermatology at the University Hospital Basel, Switzerland, between January and August 2021. It consists of high-risk patients for melanoma evaluated using 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM and 3D-Vectra WB360 total body photography devices.
classes_used: Other pigmented lesions (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma, lentigo solaris, lichenoid keratosis, folliculitis)

use.testing: The cohort served as the primary dataset for evaluating the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC) of the 2D and 3D CNNs against histopathology ground truth. A subset of 75 excised lesions was used for this accuracy analysis.
use.other: The pre-trained DEXI score model used by the FotoFinder device was trained on >66,000 photographs (external dataset not collected by the authors). The Vectra WB360 device's internal CNN algorithm details and training data were not disclosed.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Receiver operating characteristics area under the curve (ROC-AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC metrics for the 3D-CNN, 2D-CNN, dermatologists, and augmented intelligence groups.
To compare categorical variables, such as lesion classification or patient characteristics, between groups.
To compare the mean naevus counts per patient between the gold standard (medical staff) and the AI systems (3D-CNN and 2D-CNN).
To compare non-normally distributed continuous variables, such as patient age or lesion counts, between groups.
To compare categorical variables between groups.
To assess the correlation between risk assessment scores of the 2D and 3D TBP CNNs, and to evaluate the correlation between automated naevus counts and the gold standard.
To assess the diagnostic performance of the CNNs and dermatologists by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) and determining optimal cut-points for sensitivity and specificity.
To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the AI systems and dermatologists against histopathology as the ground truth.

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive Predictive Value (PPV) and Negative Predictive Value (NPV) calculation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the clinical utility of the diagnostic tests in terms of the probability of disease presence or absence given the test result.

hypothesis_testing.test: Fisher's Exact Test, Student's t-test, Pearson’s Chi-squared test, Pearson’s correlation coefficient
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in categorical variables (e.g., lesion classification, patient characteristics) between groups (Fisher's Exact Test, Chi-squared); there is no significant difference in mean naevus counts per patient between the gold standard and AI systems (Student's t-test); there is no correlation between risk assessment scores or naevus counts (Pearson's correlation).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in categorical variables between groups; there is a significant difference in mean naevus counts per patient between the gold standard and AI systems; there is a significant correlation between risk assessment scores or naevus counts.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value less than 0.05 was considered significant. The study reported significant differences in total nevi count per patient between all three methods (p < 0.0001).

Prospective Single-Centre Cohort (Univer.dataset_description: 143 high-risk patients, 1690 melanocytic lesions. Histopathology ground truth available for 75 excised lesions.
Prospective Single-Centre Cohort (Univer.evaluation_setting: Diagnostic performance against histopathology (n=75) and dermatologist assessment (n=1690).
Prospective Single-Centre Cohort (Univer.notes: The 3D-CNN outperformed the 2D-CNN. Dermatologists and augmented intelligence achieved comparable sensitivity to the 3D-CNN but superior specificity. There was a significant difference in total nevi count per patient between all three methods (p < 0.0001). The 3D-CNN showed higher correlation for repeated measurements than the 2D-CNN.

architecture_used.main_model: Two distinct commercial Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) systems: 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM and 3D-Vectra WB360 total body photography (TBP) devices.
architecture_used.pretraining: The 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM device utilizes a pre-trained DEXI score model trained on >66,000 photographs. The specific pretraining details, backbone, or training data for the 3D-Vectra WB360 internal CNN algorithm were not disclosed by the manufacturer.
architecture_used.input_size: Not reported. The devices capture total body images (2D and 3D avatar formation) which are processed by the internal CNNs.

architecture_details.classifier_head: Binary classification output (benign vs. suspicious/melanoma) with numeric risk scores.

training_details.dataset: 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM was trained on >66,000 photographs. 3D-Vectra WB360 training data was not disclosed.
training_details.testing: Evaluated on a prospective cohort of 1690 melanocytic lesions in 143 high-risk patients.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Numeric risk score and binary classification (benign/suspicious)

preprocessing_techniques: Total body photography (TBP) image capture using 2D-FotoFinder-ATBM and 3D-Vectra WB360 devices
3D avatar formation from multiple images to create a complete skin surface presentation
Manual photography of defined lesions using VISIOMED D200evo dermoscope and Medicam 1000
Application of study-specific malignancy cut-offs (FotoFinder > 0.5, Vectra > 5.0) for classification

Note: The paper reports a lack of specific preprocessing techniques like hair removal or segmentation masks in the AI algorithms, noting that factors such as hair, scarring, camera rotation, and skin markings can influence results and that the devices lack transparency regarding internal preprocessing steps.
conclusions: The study emphasizes the importance of validating convolutional neural network (CNN) classification in real-life settings rather than just under artificial conditions.
The novel 3D-CNN device (Vectra WB360) outperformed the 2D-CNN device (FotoFinder-ATBM) in diagnostic performance.
The 3D-CNN achieved comparable sensitivity (90.0%) to dermatologists and augmented intelligence (dermatologist plus AI) for melanoma detection.
Dermatologists and augmented intelligence demonstrated superior specificity (92.3% and 86.2%, respectively) compared to the 3D-CNN (64.6%).
The 2D-CNN showed lower performance with a sensitivity of 70% and specificity of 40%.
The low specificity of the CNNs currently limits the clinical utility of augmented intelligence in practice.

The automated counting of nevi by the TBP devices was inaccurate, with significant differences in mean nevi counts per patient between the gold standard (210), 3D-CNN (469), and 2D-CNN (1324), which further limits the use of augmented intelligence.

There is a lack of transparency regarding AI datasets and algorithms for skin disease, and deep learning algorithms need further optimization to avoid biases from factors such as camera rotation and pressure.

The findings should be interpreted with caution regarding generalizability due to the limited availability of the novel Vectra WB360 device and the imbalance in dermatologist experience levels in the study population.
important_remarks: The study is a single-centre, prospective observational trial conducted at the University Hospital Basel, limiting generalizability to other settings or demographics.
The cohort consisted exclusively of patients with a Caucasian genetic background, meaning results may not apply to other skin types.
The histopathology ground truth subset was small (n=75 lesions), which may produce biased and non-generalisable results; the authors acknowledge this limitation.
The number of melanomas in the ground truth subset was low (n=10), reflecting a typical limitation of prospective studies in high-risk populations.
There is a significant imbalance in dermatologist experience levels (beginners n=103, intermediate n=32, expert n=8), which may influence the assessment of augmented intelligence utility.
The 3D-Vectra WB360 device was not widely available at the start of the trial, potentially affecting the representativeness of the sample regarding device familiarity.

The AI algorithms (both 2D and 3D) lack transparency regarding their internal datasets and preprocessing steps, raising concerns about potential biases from factors like camera rotation, scarring, and hair.

The automated nevus counting by both TBP devices was inaccurate and significantly overestimated counts compared to the gold standard (3D-CNN: 469 vs. Gold Standard: 210; 2D-CNN: 1324 vs. Gold Standard: 210; p < 0.0001).
The 3D-CNN demonstrated low specificity (64.6%) compared to dermatologists (92.3%), which limits its clinical utility due to the risk of unnecessary biopsies and patient anxiety.

Augmented intelligence (dermatologist + AI) did not improve sensitivity over dermatologists alone (both 90.0%) and resulted in lower specificity (86.2% vs. 92.3%), suggesting AI assistance may reduce diagnostic accuracy in this context.
The 2D-CNN (FotoFinder-ATBM) performed poorly with a sensitivity of 70% and specificity of 40%, significantly outperformed by the 3D-CNN and dermatologists.
The study highlights that CNNs miscounted seborrhoeic keratoses, particularly in the elderly population, contributing to the inflated nevus counts.
